title: Enterprise GenAI Architecture
description: Multi-tenant platforms, access control, governance, observability, cost, and reliability.

questions.title: Design a secure enterprise RAG platform.
questions.scenario: Employees need to ask questions across HR, legal, engineering, and finance documents.
questions.expectedAnswer: A strong design includes ingestion, parsing, chunking, metadata, embeddings, indexing, ACL-filtered retrieval, reranking, prompt assembly, generation, citations, logging, evaluation, and feedback. Security includes identity, tenant isolation, encryption, audit logs, PII handling, and prompt injection defenses. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: Employees need to ask questions across HR, legal, engineering, and finance documents. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.
answerFramework.howToApply: Apply it by turning the concept into architecture: define inputs, data flow, ownership boundaries, controls, evaluation signals, observability, and rollout steps for enterprise genai architecture.
answerFramework.example: Example: when asked "Design a secure enterprise RAG platform.", use the scenario to show where the idea applies, what you would do first, what result you expect, and how you would know the decision worked in a real enterprise genai architecture setting.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: Mention tradeoffs explicitly: quality versus cost, latency versus completeness, autonomy versus control, simplicity versus flexibility, and where human review or governance is needed. Then close with how you would measure success.

questions.title: Why would an enterprise use a model gateway?
questions.scenario: Many teams independently call different LLM providers with no shared governance.
questions.expectedAnswer: A model gateway centralizes model access, routing, authentication, authorization, logging, rate limits, cost tracking, retries, fallbacks, caching, and policy enforcement. It reduces duplicated integrations and gives platform teams control over quality, latency, and spend. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: Many teams independently call different LLM providers with no shared governance. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.

questions.title: How do you design multi-tenancy for GenAI platforms?
questions.scenario: A SaaS company wants one GenAI platform for many customers.
questions.expectedAnswer: Multi-tenancy requires tenant isolation in storage, indexes, prompts, logs, credentials, rate limits, and access policies. Strong designs include separate encryption keys where needed, tenant-aware retrieval filters, quota management, audit logs, and tests for cross-tenant leakage. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: A SaaS company wants one GenAI platform for many customers. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.

questions.title: How would you reduce GenAI cost without damaging quality?
questions.scenario: An internal assistant becomes expensive as usage grows.
questions.expectedAnswer: Use model routing, prompt compression, caching, retrieval precision, smaller models for simple tasks, batching, token budgets, and output length controls. Measure cost per successful task and evaluate quality after each optimization. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: An internal assistant becomes expensive as usage grows. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.

questions.title: What governance does an enterprise GenAI platform need?
questions.scenario: Executives want many teams to build GenAI apps safely and consistently.
questions.expectedAnswer: Governance includes model approval, prompt review, data access policies, tool permissions, audit logs, safety standards, evaluation gates, incident response, vendor risk management, and documentation. It should enable teams without creating uncontrolled risk. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

questions.scenario: An interviewer asks you to reason through tenant-aware observability for an enterprise AI use case where correctness, cost, security, and adoption all matter.
questions.expectedAnswer: A strong answer should define tenant-aware observability, explain why it matters for enterprise genai architecture, and connect it to a practical implementation decision. The candidate should describe the core mechanism, identify where it appears in a production system, discuss tradeoffs such as quality, cost, latency, safety, governance, and maintainability, and name how they would evaluate whether the approach is working. For senior or architect-level interviews, the answer should also include failure modes, ownership boundaries, and rollout considerations. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: An interviewer asks you to reason through tenant-aware observability for an enterprise AI use case where correctness, cost, security, and adoption all matter. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.
answerFramework.howToApply: Apply it by turning the concept into architecture: define inputs, data flow, ownership boundaries, controls, evaluation signals, observability, and rollout steps for enterprise genai architecture.
answerFramework.example: Example: when asked "How would you explain tenant-aware observability in a Enterprise GenAI Architecture interview?", use the scenario to show where the idea applies, what you would do first, what result you expect, and how you would know the decision worked in a real enterprise genai architecture setting.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: Mention tradeoffs explicitly: quality versus cost, latency versus completeness, autonomy versus control, simplicity versus flexibility, and where human review or governance is needed. Then close with how you would measure success.
